title: HTTP Protocol Engineering in Go 1.26: Bytes to HTTP/3

audience.description: Go service developers who can build production HTTP clients and servers and now need byte-level protocol, parser, interoperability, and security mastery.
entryKnowledge: Build and defend production Go 1.26 HTTP origin services with lifecycle, semantics, limits, tests, observability, and recovery.
Build robust Go HTTP clients with context, streaming, retries, TLS, redirects, and explicit trust boundaries.
deviceConstraints: Modern browser; learner Go runs only in the isolated deterministic Go worker. Browser tasks model octets, framing, parser states, and protocol decisions without opening sockets, contacting networks, reading host files, using credentials, or negotiating live TLS.
accessibilityAssumptions: Byte diagrams, protocol state machines, frame layouts, packet traces, timelines, and parser tables include equivalent structured text and keyboard-operable evidence.

placementEvidence: A placement review or prior artifact must demonstrate this entry skill: Build and defend production Go 1.26 HTTP origin services with lifecycle, semantics, limits, tests, observability, and recovery.
A placement review or prior artifact must demonstrate this entry skill: Build robust Go HTTP clients with context, streaming, retries, TLS, redirects, and explicit trust boundaries.
completionEvidence: Complete and defend all 5 stakeholder projects against their acceptance constraints and rubrics.
Pass the cumulative performance examination at or above 85 percent with changed-case evidence.

includes: Go 1.26.5 octet and stream handling, partial I/O, TCP lifecycle, strict bounded HTTP/1.1 parsing and serialization, request targets, fields, framing, fixed and chunked content, trailers, response control data, persistence, expectations, intermediaries, authority, TLS 1.3, ALPN, Upgrade, CONNECT, request smuggling and response splitting defense, slow-client limits, state machines, differential and fuzz testing, HTTP/2 frames and HPACK, HTTP/3 QUIC and QPACK, deployment, and incident defense

Runnable deterministic pure-Go protocol decision models plus explicit compilation, net.Pipe, loopback socket, TLS, proxy, packet-capture, fuzz, race, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production transfer gates
Five cumulative protocol-engineering projects and a performance-based certification exam
excludes: Replacing maintained Go net/http, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, TLS, or QUIC implementations in production with training parsers
Browser execution of sockets, listeners, host processes, packet capture, live TLS, proxies, external networks, credentials, or untrusted code

sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves Go, HTTP client and server, accessibility, security, testing, context, cleanup, and evidence habits before adding one protocol boundary.

Browser Go is deterministic and isolated. It models octets, framing, state, policy, and changed cases but never opens sockets, negotiates TLS, contacts networks or proxies, captures packets, executes host commands, reads host state, or handles credentials.

Passing work requires exact bytes or state input, a named invariant and owner, bounded resource and time policy, observable changed and failure results, cleanup and recovery, and explicit full-toolchain transfer limits.

competencies.statement: Update framing, validators, digests, content codings, warnings, and signatures when an intermediary transforms content.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Update framing, validators, digests, content codings, warnings, and signatures when an intermediary transforms content.

competencies.statement: Construct trusted Via and forwarding evidence at controlled hops without accepting client-supplied identity as authoritative.
misconceptions: Appending one trusted value makes all earlier X-Forwarded values trustworthy.

competencies.statement: Assign parse, policy, connect, timeout, upstream, transform, and downstream-write failures to the correct hop and safe response boundary.

competencies.statement: Require exactly one valid Host field for HTTP/1.1 where applicable and reject missing, empty, duplicate, or invalid authority.

competencies.statement: Reconstruct the effective request URI from target form, request-target, Host, connection security, and trusted configuration.

competencies.statement: Handle proxy absolute-form authority and Host according to RFC rules while rejecting unsafe disagreement.
misconceptions: When absolute-form and Host disagree, whichever appears first should control routing.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Handle proxy absolute-form authority and Host according to RFC rules while rejecting unsafe disagreement.

competencies.statement: Normalize host and port only as specified, validate against configured authority, then route, cache, authorize tenants, or construct links.

competencies.statement: Validate certificate chain, time, DNS or IP reference identity, key usage, revocation policy, and trust roots without insecure skip flags.
misconceptions: A certificate signed by any trusted root is valid for every requested hostname.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Validate certificate chain, time, DNS or IP reference identity, key usage, revocation policy, and trust roots without insecure skip flags.

competencies.statement: Offer and select application protocols with ALPN before application data and reject unsupported or inconsistent negotiation.
misconceptions: A server can inspect the first HTTP request to decide retroactively whether ALPN selected h2.

competencies.statement: Define supported versions, cipher and curve policy, session resumption, client authentication, and replay boundaries while treating early data explicitly.

competencies.statement: Validate Connection upgrade, Upgrade tokens, request completion, status 101, selected protocol, and ownership transfer before switching parsers.

competencies.statement: Compare raw and decoded targets, path separators, percent encodings, authority, query boundaries, and routing between policy and downstream recipients.
misconceptions: If two parsers produce the same visible path, they necessarily enforce the same request boundary.

competencies.statement: Prevent attacker-controlled CRLF and control bytes from creating new fields, responses, logs, caches, or downstream protocol records.
misconceptions: HTML escaping is sufficient to prevent HTTP response splitting.

competencies.statement: Test HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 to HTTP/1.1 translation, proxy chains, cache parsing, and backend normalization with the same adversarial corpus.

competencies.statement: Bound start-line, header, content, trailer, idle, and total-phase time using both progress and absolute budgets.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Bound start-line, header, content, trailer, idle, and total-phase time using both progress and absolute budgets.

competencies.statement: Bound per-connection buffers, retained slices, field maps, content queues, trailer data, and pooled-object capacity before allocation.
misconceptions: A bounded content body guarantees total memory per connection is bounded.

competencies.statement: Propagate slow peer writes through bounded queues, cancellation, deadlines, partial output evidence, and producer shutdown.

competencies.statement: Limit connections, active parsers, work, tenant share, and output while rejecting early and preserving fairness and health evidence.
misconceptions: A single global connection limit guarantees fair service for every tenant and request cost.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Limit connections, active parsers, work, tenant share, and output while rejecting early and preserving fairness and health evidence.

competencies.statement: Prove connection, goroutine, timer, buffer, queue, tunnel, temporary data, and metric-cardinality cleanup for every terminal state.
misconceptions: Defers guarantee cleanup finishes within budget after every timeout and forced shutdown.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Prove connection, goroutine, timer, buffer, queue, tunnel, temporary data, and metric-cardinality cleanup for every terminal state.

competencies.statement: Represent protocol phase, required input, transition, emitted event, retained buffer, limit, and terminal result explicitly across fragmented reads.
misconceptions: A parser that returns need-more-data has no state worth testing separately.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Represent protocol phase, required input, transition, emitted event, retained buffer, limit, and terminal result explicitly across fragmented reads.

competencies.statement: Define invariants for cursor bounds, monotonic consumption, one framing choice, one response order, one close owner, and no effects before validation.
misconceptions: Valid output examples are stronger evidence than invariants over every parser transition.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Define invariants for cursor bounds, monotonic consumption, one framing choice, one response order, one close owner, and no effects before validation.

competencies.statement: Assign one coordinated reader and ordered writer per HTTP/1.1 connection with explicit handoff for tunnels and shutdown.
misconceptions: Multiple goroutines may read one TCP stream safely because each request has a separate boundary.

competencies.statement: Arbitrate parse, application, peer, timeout, cancellation, write, and shutdown failures so exactly one terminal decision owns response and cleanup.
misconceptions: The last goroutine to report an error should choose the connection outcome.

competencies.statement: Record low-cardinality phase, framing source, bytes, duration, terminal reason, peer class, and correlation without raw secrets or attacker-controlled labels.
misconceptions: Logging the entire raw message is the only way to diagnose parser state failures.

competencies.statement: Use exact byte fixtures for valid, incomplete, malformed, normalized, serialized, and residual-buffer cases with readable semantic assertions.
misconceptions: Golden bytes should replace semantic assertions because byte equality proves every behavior.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use exact byte fixtures for valid, incomplete, malformed, normalized, serialized, and residual-buffer cases with readable semantic assertions.

competencies.statement: Use net.Pipe or a scripted short reader and writer to test fragmentation, blocking, deadlines, half-close, cancellation, and cleanup deterministically.
misconceptions: Loopback TCP reliably produces every fragmentation and short-write schedule needed for tests.

competencies.statement: Compare supported inputs with Go net/http and independent recipients while treating deliberate strictness and implementation quirks as reviewed differences.

competencies.statement: Fuzz parser and serializer with seed corpora, fragmentation, invariants, round trips, bounds, no panic, no hang, and retained minimized failures.
misconceptions: A fuzz target is useful only when it discovers a crash rather than a semantic or resource invariant violation.

competencies.statement: Run race, leak, allocation, benchmark, profiling, and changed-load gates while separating correctness from capacity claims.
misconceptions: Passing the race detector and a fast microbenchmark proves production protocol safety and capacity.

competencies.statement: Map pseudo-fields and regular fields, enforce ordering and connection-field rules, bound HPACK tables and header lists, and avoid indexing secrets.
misconceptions: HPACK compression is stateless per request and cannot expose cross-stream information.

misconceptions: Advertising h3 causes every client to switch immediately and permanently.

projects.title: Fragment-Safe HTTP/1.1 Request Parser
projects.stakeholder: A municipal gateway and assistive-client integration team
projects.userNeed: They need a strict bounded Go parser that preserves layer evidence, handles fragmented TCP bytes, enforces deadlines, separates semantics from syntax, applies ABNF limits, and parses all request-target forms without side effects.
constraints: Every input, assumption, transformation, and result is traceable.
Invalid, boundary, and changed cases are tested instead of silently discarded.
The result is understandable and useful to its named stakeholder.
